s: London's only surviving double-decker stable
w: Built in 1797 by James Burton to rest ailing horses en route to the city — the concrete ramps inside, grooved for grip, still slope between floors. Became an underground arts venue in 1993 and never really recovered from that sentence. Grade II listed. Still weird.
_facts: Built 1797 by James Burton
London's only surviving double-decker inner-city stable with concrete ramps
Ramps have cross-slat grooves for horse grip between floors
Became an independent arts venue in 1993
Grade II listed

s: The City's only pre-Fire tavern still trading
w: A Martin Lane cellar that opened before Charles II's navy had a single ship ready for war — and survived both the Great Fire of 1666 and the Blitz. Charles Dickens drank here. The ceilings are low, the wine list is serious, and history is the house speciality.
_facts: Opened circa 1663, before the Great Fire of 1666
One of very few buildings in the City to survive the Great Fire
Also survived WWII bombing of the City
Charles Dickens was a known visitor
Still operating as a wine bar

s: Blitz survivor granted by Henry V post-Agincourt
w: Henry V granted the Cutlers their charter after Agincourt — the French couldn't have fought without English blades. The Warwick Lane hall is the only livery building in the City to survive the 1940 Blitz alongside St Paul's. The frieze was cut by a sculptor who started as a Sheffield knife-grinder's boy.
_facts: Charter granted 1416 by Henry V after Agincourt
One of only two major buildings in that part of the City to survive the 1940 Blitz (alongside St Paul's)
Elephant and castle arms reference ivory handle-making trade
Terracotta frieze sculptor grew up as knife-grinder apprentice in Sheffield
Arms feature a sword, elephant and castle

s: Gilded fat boy marks where the Great Fire died
w: The Great Fire started in Pudding Lane and burnt itself out here, at Pye Corner — so the Victorians put up a gilded chubby boy with an inscription blaming the catastrophe on the 'Sin of Gluttony'. Food started it, food ended it. The Monument marks the origin; this small gold figure marks the death.
_facts: Marks the exact spot where the Great Fire of London ended in 1666
The fire started in Pudding Lane; the coincidence with Pye Corner led to the 'Gluttony' inscription
Originally mounted on the Fortune of War tavern, now on a wall at the corner of Giltspur Street and Cock Lane
The gilded wooden boy dates from the 18th century
Grade II listed

s: Wren spire exiled to a suburban garden
w: When the Corporation of London demolished St Antholin's Watling Street in 1874, a local vicar rescued Christopher Wren's elegant stone spire from the rubble and re-erected it in a South London garden. It stands in a residential street in Sydenham still — a 17th-century City church tower marooned among Victorian villas.
_facts: Original church built by Wren after the Great Fire of 1666
Church demolished by City of London 1874 to widen road
Spire relocated to a garden in Sydenham by Reverend Mortlock
Grade II listed structure
One of London's most obscure Wren survivals

s: A Victorian gasometer ring, now a park
w: Three cast-iron gasholders stood here from the 1850s. The guide frames — 240 tonnes of Victorian ironwork — were dismantled, shot-blasted, and rebuilt as a circular park. The Regent's Canal laps one edge. Inside the ring: lawn, benches, a fountain. Outside: the most photographed backdrop in King's Cross.
_facts: Gasholders built 1850s by Imperial Gas Light and Coke Company
Gasholders 8, 10 and 12 were listed structures
Iron guide frames (240 tonnes each) dismantled, restored, and reassembled as park enclosure
Opened as public park 2017
Designed by Gasworks developer King's Cross Central and landscape architects Bell Phillips

s: Named for a lady found torn apart at a ball
w: Legend says Lady Elizabeth Hatton was discovered here in January 1626, her heart still bleeding in the cobbles — torn apart by the Devil at a Hatton Garden ball. More likely a disputed estate. The courtyard is real, the atmosphere is vintage London. The story is better than the facts.
_facts: Hidden yard off Greville Street in Holborn
Named for a 17th-century legend about Lady Elizabeth Hatton, found dead with a 'bleeding heart' in the yard
The Hatton Garden area was historically a Huguenot settlement
Dickens set scenes here in Little Dorrit
Site of a long-running bistro and pub named after the yard

s: London's finest Georgian shopfront, barely noticed
w: Built 1750-56 for Huguenot silk merchants Jourdain and Rybot — possibly by Sir Robert Taylor — this is the most complete mid-Georgian shopfront in London. Five Doric columns, a carved fascia, Venetian window above. The interior period rooms were shipped to the Art Institute of Chicago in 1927. The facade stayed.
_facts: Grade I listed; considered the finest mid-Georgian shopfront in London
Built 1750-56 for Huguenot silk merchants Nicolas Jourdain and Francis Rybot
Architect possibly Sir Robert Taylor
Features five Doric columns and a carved fascia
Interior period rooms removed and now in the Art Institute of Chicago (acquired 1927)

s: The most complete Georgian street in London
w: Two terraces of 1720s-30s houses between Norton Folgate and Fleur de Lis Street — built when Spitalfields was Huguenot silk-weaving country. The area faced slum clearance in the 1970s; residents fought and won. Grade II listed streetscape. A ghost of Georgian London, walkable and mostly unchanged.
_facts: Dates to 1720s-1730s, Huguenot silk-weaver period in Spitalfields
One of the best-preserved Georgian residential streets in London
Threatened with demolition in 1970s before preservation campaigns
Home to several notable residents over the centuries
Grade II listed as a complete streetscape

s: The City church Betjeman called least-spoiled
w: Thomas Tallis was organist here in 1538. Wren rebuilt the interior after the Great Fire but left the 12th-century walls standing — archaeologists have since found Roman and Anglo-Saxon layers beneath. Betjeman called it 'the least spoiled interior in the City'. Skulls carved above the passageway. Hidden off Lovat Lane.
_facts: Founded 12th century; one of the oldest churches in the City
Thomas Tallis served as organist 1538-39
Wren rebuilt the interior post-1666 but retained medieval walls
Archaeological layers include Roman and Anglo-Saxon finds
John Betjeman described it as 'the least spoiled interior in the City of London'

s: Largest intact Roman villa in Greater London
w: A working Roman farm from the 2nd century AD, with underfloor hypocaust heating still readable in its footprint. The largest accessible Roman villa in Greater London — and the least visited. Open weekends, hidden inside a housing estate in Orpington. The floors that warmed a Roman family are where they left them.
_facts: Dating from 2nd century AD
Features underfloor hypocaust heating system
Largest intact Roman villa site in Greater London
Open to public at weekends (managed by London Borough of Bromley)
Located within a housing estate in Orpington, BR6

s: William Morris built his dream house and left it
w: Philip Webb designed this red-brick house for William Morris in 1859 — the two of them inventing what became the Arts and Crafts movement in the process. Morris lived here 1860-65, then abandoned it when the commute to London became too much. National Trust now. The garden is resolutely medieval.
_facts: Designed by Philip Webb for William Morris in 1859
Morris lived here from 1860-1865
Considered the first Arts and Crafts building in England
National Trust property open to the public
Gardens designed by Morris in a medieval style; original well-head survives

s: Chalk grassland hidden in Islington's back streets
w: A decommissioned railway siding in Islington that nature quietly colonised. Five hectares of chalk grassland, secondary woodland, and scrub — habitats you'd normally travel to the Chilterns to find. SSSI designated. Ranger-led events in season. The path emerges on Drayton Park Road and you wonder where you've been.
_facts: Former Great Northern Railway goods siding, closed and abandoned
Designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI)
Contains chalk grassland habitats unusual for inner London
5 hectares in size
Managed by London Borough of Islington with a full-time ecology centre

s: Soho's marble-lined pool with LGBTQ+ history
w: Opened 1931, lined with Sicilian and Swedish green marble, a bronze fountain at the shallow end. The Marshall Street baths became an important LGBTQ+ meeting place in the decades when that carried real risk. Closed 1997, the council nearly sold it off; a campaign saved it and it reopened 2010 with the marble intact.
_facts: Opened 1931, designed by Alfred and Kenneth Cross for Westminster City Council
Interior lined with Sicilian marble and Swedish green marble
Original bronze fountain at the shallow end survives
Served as significant LGBTQ+ venue during the 20th century
Closed 1997 for structural reasons; reopened 2010 after restoration

s: Oldest livery hall on its original site
w: The Fishmongers have stood on this bank of the Thames since the 13th century — the only livery company on its original site. A Fishmonger Lord Mayor killed Wat Tyler with a dagger at Smithfield in 1381; the dagger is still here. The hall overlooks the bridge and has Roman wharf timbers beneath its foundations.
_facts: Fishmongers' Company on this Thames site since at least 1310
Only City livery company continuously on its original site
Lord Mayor William Walworth (a Fishmonger) killed Wat Tyler at Smithfield in 1381; his dagger displayed in the hall
Roman wharf archaeology beneath current building
Current hall designed by Henry Roberts, 1831-34

s: Brixton's last great Caribbean record shop
w: Wally Bryant opened Supertone on Acre Lane in 1984 — the shop grew from a soundsystem founded in 1969 that rocked Brixton long before the riots. Reggae, roots, dub, dancehall, lovers rock: every pressing, every sleeve. One of the last specialist Caribbean music shops in south London. Still going.
_facts: Founded as a soundsystem in 1969
Record shop opened 1984 by Wally Bryant on Acre Lane, SW2
Specialist in reggae, roots, dub, dancehall, and Caribbean music
Icon of Black British music culture in Brixton
One of the last remaining specialist Caribbean record shops in London

s: Where Guernica came to London for a pair of boots
w: Founded 1901 to bring great art to the East End; when Picasso's Guernica came for its only UK showing in 1939, the entrance fee was a pair of boots donated to the Spanish Republic. The Art Nouveau building launched Hockney, Freud, Kapoor. Still free. The boots story alone earns it a spot.
_facts: Founded 1901 by Canon and Henrietta Barnett
Guernica exhibited here in 1939 — the only UK showing; admission was a pair of boots for Spanish Republic aid
Art Nouveau building by Charles Harrison Townsend
Helped launch major British artists including David Hockney, Lucian Freud, and Anish Kapoor
Free admission; expanded into neighbouring library building in 2009

s: 65,000 books on type, ink, and the press
w: When the newspapers left Fleet Street, their typefaces stayed — here, in the world's largest specialist collection of printing and typography. Opened 1895. Original punches from the Caslon and Figgins foundries, 65,000 books, type specimens no one else preserved. Down a passage off Bride Lane.
_facts: Opened 1895 as a technical library for the printing trade
World's largest specialist collection on printing and typography
Holds 65,000 books and original type punches from Caslon and Figgins foundries
Became de facto repository of English typographic heritage as newspapers left Fleet Street
Located at 14 Bride Lane, EC4Y (same building as Bridewell Theatre)

s: World's oldest Holocaust archive, open to all
w: Alfred Wiener began collecting documents on Nazi persecution in Berlin in the late 1920s, moved them to Amsterdam, then London. The library arrived in 1939 and never left. Now at Russell Square: over a million items — testimony, documents, photographs — open to any researcher, no appointment needed.
_facts: Founded by Alfred Wiener in 1920s Berlin
Moved to Amsterdam 1933, then London 1939
World's oldest Holocaust library and archive
Holds over 1 million items documenting the Holocaust and genocide
Open to the public without charge; relocated to Russell Square in 2011

s: Blow Up Bridge — the 1874 gunpowder explosion
w: On 2 October 1874 a gunpowder barge exploded beneath this canal bridge — four killed, blast heard 12 miles away, the Zoo animals panicked for hours. Bridge rebuilt from the salvage; the columns reportedly went back in upside-down. Parliament passed the Explosives Act 1875 as a direct result.
_facts: Explosion of gunpowder barge Tilbury on 2 October 1874 killed 4 people
Blast heard 10-12 miles away; damaged hundreds of buildings
London Zoo animals alarmed for hours
Bridge rebuilt from recovered ironwork — the columns were reportedly reassembled inverted
Parliament passed Explosives Act 1875 partly as a result of the disaster

s: Morris's own boyhood home, now a world collection
w: William Morris's family moved to Water House in 1848 when he was 14; he left at 19 and kept designing for six more decades. The Georgian villa now holds the world's largest public collection of his work — textiles, tiles, furniture, drawings. Clement Attlee opened it in 1950. Won Museum of the Year in 2013. Free.
_facts: Water House was the Morris family home 1848-1856 (William Morris aged 14-22)
Georgian villa with a moat predating the house
World's largest public collection of William Morris's work
Opened as museum in 1950 by Prime Minister Clement Attlee
Won Art Fund Museum of the Year 2013

s: Ad ban dodged by building the letters into the tower
w: Liebig (Oxo) bought a Royal Mail power station in the 1920s and needed a sign. The LCC refused. The architect designed tower windows in O-X-O pattern instead — architecture, not advertising, technically. The LCC allowed it. Tower built 1929. Nobody was fooled and nobody cared.
_facts: Building originally constructed as a power station for the Royal Mail
Acquired by Liebig Extract of Meat Company (makers of OXO) in 1920s
London County Council refused permission for illuminated advertising signs
Architect designed windows in OXO pattern (circle-cross-circle) to circumvent ban
Tower built 1929; now home to independent design studios and a restaurant

s: CAMRA-listed Courage tap, hidden off Tower Bridge
w: The tap room to the Courage Anchor Brewery that once dominated Horsleydown — tucked into a back street behind Tower Bridge. Samuel Smith's bought it in the early 1990s and kept the Victorian bar counter and tiled fireplaces untouched. CAMRA National Inventory. You have to know it's there.
_facts: Built as the tap house to the Courage Anchor Brewery, Horsleydown
Sold to Samuel Smith Old Brewery (Tadcaster) in the early 1990s
Grade II listed building
Included in CAMRA's National Inventory of Historic Pub Interiors
Victorian bar counter, tiled fireplaces and original fittings intact

s: World's oldest Dutch church, granted by Edward VI in 1550
w: Edward VI granted the nave of a medieval Augustinian friary to Protestant refugees from the Low Countries in 1550 — making this the first officially sanctioned nonconformist chapel in England. The original building was destroyed in the Blitz on 15 October 1940; the rebuilt church holds services in Dutch to this day.
_facts: Founded 1550 when Edward VI granted the nave of Austin Friars to Dutch and German Protestant refugees
First officially sanctioned nonconformist chapel in England
Original medieval friary building destroyed in Blitz on 15 October 1940
Rebuilt 1950-54
Martin Droeshout (engraver of First Folio Shakespeare portrait) was descended from the congregation

s: Turner Prize winners converted Victorian baths to gallery
w: An 1898 public baths closed in 1991, left empty, then handed to Assemble — the architecture collective who won the Turner Prize in 2015. They built a gallery inside it: cast-iron tank panels as walls, turquoise concrete echoing the original tiles. Opened 2018. The pool is gone but the room still holds its shape.
_facts: Original building: 1898 Victorian public baths
Baths closed 1991
Converted by Assemble architecture collective (Turner Prize winners 2015)
Cast-iron water tank panels incorporated into gallery walls
Opened as gallery 2018

s: London's oldest canal, dug in 16 months
w: Authorised 1767, opened 17 September 1770 — 1.4 miles from Bow Locks to the Thames at Limehouse Basin, dug in 16 months to bypass the tidal Lea. London's oldest canal, still navigable. Factories once lined every inch of bank; now the towpath runs between converted warehouses and the A12.
_facts: Opened 17 September 1770 — oldest canal in the London area
Authorised by River Lea Act 1767
1.4 miles (2.25 km) from Bow Locks to Limehouse Basin
Dug in approximately 16 months
Bypassed the tidal River Lea; still fully navigable

s: Site of Britain's first gay rights demo, 1970
w: On 27 November 1970, 150 Gay Liberation Front members gathered here with torches to protest the arrest of Louis Eaks for 'importuning' — Britain's first organised gay rights demo. The Peasants' Revolt mustered on the same fields in 1381. Islington parkland. More history than it looks.
_facts: On 27 November 1970, 150 Gay Liberation Front members held torchlight rally here — first organised gay rights demonstration in Britain
Protest against arrest of Louis Eaks for 'importuning'
The GLF called such events 'zaps'
Peasants' Revolt connection: Sir Robert Hales (who imposed the poll tax) lived at Highbury Manor
27-acre public park in Islington, N5

s: England's first gated community, in Gothic Revival
w: Baroness Angela Burdett-Coutts — one of the wealthiest women in Victorian Britain — commissioned eight Gothic fantasy cottages around a central green from Henry Astley Darbishire in 1865. Still a private estate behind an ornate archway. Claimed to be England's first gated community. You peer through the gates.
_facts: Eight Gothic Revival cottages built 1865 by Baroness Angela Burdett-Coutts
Designed by architect Henry Astley Darbishire
Burdett-Coutts built it as model housing for deserving workers
Still a private residential estate with restricted access
Claimed to be England's first purpose-built gated community

s: Victorian water filter beds, returned to nature
w: Six filter beds built in 1852 by the East London Waterworks Company after cholera outbreaks — purifying River Lea water for east London. Decommissioned 1969; nature moved in. Now a Lee Valley wildlife reserve: 200 plant species, 60 bird species, the brick filter walls still standing.
_facts: Six filter beds built 1852 by East London Waterworks Company post-cholera
Purified River Lea water for east London
Decommissioned 1969
Managed as wildlife reserve by Lee Valley Regional Park since 1988
Over 200 plant species and 60 bird species; original brick filter structures intact

s: Edith Nesbit's moated manor, now a public park
w: Well Hall estate has a moat recorded in the 13th century; Margaret Roper, daughter of Thomas More, lived here after 1521. Edith Nesbit — who wrote The Railway Children — made it her home from 1899 to 1921, hosting literary parties in the Tudor Barn that still stands. The park opened 1933. The barn is now a restaurant.
_facts: Well Hall estate recorded 13th century with moated manor
Home of Margaret Roper (daughter of Thomas More) after 1521
Edith Nesbit (author of The Railway Children, Five Children and It) lived at Well Hall House 1899-1921
Medieval Tudor Barn survives as the main standing structure
Public park opened 1933; Edith Nesbit Walk named within the grounds
